purpose: Provides decade-by-decade historical context for KinLore narrative generation. The agent uses this to ground stories in accurate period details — economics, social norms, daily life, and dramatic events.

key_events: Pierre Le Moyne d'Iberville establishes the first French settlement at Dauphin Island (1699) and the Mobile Bay area
Jean-Baptiste Le Moyne de Bienville founds Fort Louis de la Mobile on the Mobile River (1702)
Mobile serves as the capital of French Louisiana (1702–1720)
French missionaries and traders establish relations with the Choctaw, Chickasaw, and Creek (Muscogee) nations
Yellow fever outbreak strikes the Mobile settlement (1704–1705)
First enslaved Africans brought to the Mobile colony by French traders (early 1700s)
1700s.economic_conditions: The French colony at Mobile was a struggling outpost dependent on supply ships from France and trade with Native nations. There was no profitable cash crop; the colony survived on the fur and deerskin trade with the Choctaw and Creek. France poured money into Louisiana with little return. Colonists bartered with Native peoples and relied heavily on corn, game, and fish for subsistence.
1700s.migration_patterns: Settlers were a small number of French soldiers, traders, missionaries, and engages (contract laborers) sent by the French crown. Canadian coureurs de bois and voyageurs also traveled south. Enslaved Africans arrived in small numbers. The region remained overwhelmingly populated by Native nations — the Creek (Muscogee), Choctaw, Chickasaw, and Cherokee — who vastly outnumbered Europeans.
1700s.social_context: The French colonists lived in close proximity and frequent intermarriage with Native peoples, creating a mixed Franco-Native-African society distinct from English colonial patterns. Catholicism was the official religion, brought by Jesuit and later Capuchin missionaries. Enslaved Africans and Native peoples occupied the lowest rungs of French colonial hierarchy, but social boundaries were more fluid than in English colonies. The Code Noir (1724) would later formalize slave regulations.
1700s.daily_life_snapshot: Colonists in Mobile lived in simple wooden structures surrounded by a palisade, eating corn, game, fish, and whatever could be traded from the Choctaw. Tropical diseases — malaria, yellow fever, dysentery — were constant threats. Social life revolved around the Catholic church, the military garrison, and trade gatherings with Native allies. Women were scarce in the early colony, leading to unions between French men and Native or African women.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: Yellow fever epidemic devastates the small Mobile settlement (1704–1705)
Chronic supply shortages from France leave colonists near starvation
Hurricane damage to the Gulf Coast settlement (1700s)
Conflict between French and English over trade alliances with Native nations
narrative_hooks: A French soldier at Fort Louis de la Mobile watches a Choctaw trading party arrive with deerskins, knowing the colony's survival depends on this relationship
An enslaved African woman in Mobile navigates a world where French, Native, and African cultures mix in ways unknown in the English colonies
A Catholic missionary struggles to convert Creek villagers who see no reason to abandon their own spiritual traditions

key_events: Mobile settlement relocated to present-day downtown Mobile site after flooding (1711)
Fort Conde constructed at the new Mobile site, becoming the principal French fortification (1711–1723)
French-allied Choctaw and French-opposed Chickasaw nations drawn into proxy conflicts tied to European rivalries
Capital of French Louisiana shifted from Mobile to Biloxi (1719) and then New Orleans (1720)
Natchez and Yazoo peoples in the broader Louisiana territory engage in periodic conflicts with French
John Law's Company of the West (Mississippi Company) takes over Louisiana colonization (1717)
1710s.economic_conditions: The colony remained economically marginal. The fur and deerskin trade with Native nations was the primary source of value. John Law's Mississippi Company attempted to attract settlers and investment with wildly exaggerated promises of wealth, but the colony produced little exportable goods. The shift of the capital away from Mobile in 1720 reduced the town's importance. Small-scale farming and livestock raising supplemented trade.
1710s.migration_patterns: The Mississippi Company recruited settlers from France, Germany, and Switzerland with promises of land and opportunity. Most were deposited at the Gulf Coast and left to fend for themselves. Some German families settled along the Mobile and Tombigbee rivers. Enslaved Africans arrived in increasing numbers aboard French slave ships. Native nations remained the dominant population throughout the region.
1710s.social_context: French colonial society in Alabama remained small, multiracial, and heavily dependent on alliances with Native nations. Intermarriage between French men and Native or African women continued. The Catholic Church provided what little institutional structure existed beyond the military garrison. The arrival of more enslaved Africans began shifting the labor system. Native political alliances — particularly the French-Choctaw partnership against the English-allied Chickasaw — shaped regional power dynamics.
1710s.daily_life_snapshot: Residents of Mobile lived in a small town clustered around Fort Conde, the church, and the waterfront. Diet relied on corn, rice (introduced), fish, oysters, and wild game traded from Native allies. Houses were modest wooden structures adapted to the subtropical climate with raised floors and wide galleries. Mosquitoes, heat, and humidity made summers miserable. Social gatherings centered on religious feast days, military ceremonies, and trade fairs.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: Flooding forces relocation of Mobile (1711)
Chronic supply shortages and French governmental neglect
Mississippi Company's speculative bubble raises and dashes hopes (1717–1720)
Endemic malaria and yellow fever in the Gulf lowlands
narrative_hooks: A German family recruited by the Mississippi Company arrives at Mobile Bay to find swamp and mosquitoes instead of the paradise they were promised
A Choctaw headman negotiates with French officers at Fort Conde, balancing alliance with France against English pressure from Carolina
A French trader's Choctaw wife mediates between two worlds, translating language and culture at the Mobile trading post

key_events: Capital of French Louisiana permanently established at New Orleans (1720), diminishing Mobile's administrative role
Code Noir formally applied to Louisiana territory, regulating slavery and racial categories (1724)
Collapse of John Law's Mississippi Bubble devastates investment in the colony (1720)
Fort Toulouse (Fort Toulouse des Alibamons) established among the Upper Creek at the confluence of the Coosa and Tallapoosa rivers (1717, expanded 1720s)
French and Choctaw alliance deepens as counterweight to English and Chickasaw
Small numbers of enslaved Africans work on farms and in households around Mobile
1720s.economic_conditions: The collapse of the Mississippi Bubble in 1720 ended speculative investment in Louisiana. The colony reverted to crown control and languished economically. Mobile survived on the deerskin trade and subsistence agriculture. The Code Noir of 1724 formalized slavery but also granted enslaved people limited rights (Sundays off, prohibition on family separation — often ignored in practice). The interior fur trade remained the colony's economic lifeline.
1720s.migration_patterns: Immigration from France slowed dramatically after the Mississippi Bubble collapse. The existing Franco-African-Native population grew through natural increase and small continued importations of enslaved Africans. Interior Alabama remained firmly under Native control — the Creek Confederacy dominated the central and eastern regions, the Choctaw the southwest, and the Chickasaw the northwest. French presence was confined to Mobile and scattered interior trading posts.
1720s.social_context: The Code Noir codified a racial hierarchy but also created a recognized category of free people of color (gens de couleur libres) who would become an important community in Mobile. French-Native intermarriage continued, producing a metis population. The Catholic Church remained the sole organized religious institution. Social life was intimate and stratified — a small French elite, a mixed-race middle layer, enslaved Africans at the bottom, and surrounding Native nations operating in their own sovereign systems.
1720s.daily_life_snapshot: Mobile was a sleepy colonial town of perhaps a few hundred residents, clustered around Fort Conde and the church. People ate rice, corn, fish, shellfish, and wild game. Trade with the Creek and Choctaw brought deerskins that were shipped to France. The subtropical climate dictated daily rhythms — early rising, midday rest, evening socializing on galleries. Enslaved people worked in households, gardens, and on small farms. Festivals, religious observances, and the arrival of ships from France were the main social events.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: Mississippi Bubble collapse wipes out investment and settler confidence (1720)
Continued yellow fever and malaria epidemics in the Gulf lowlands
French crown neglect leaves the colony underfunded and undersupplied
Periodic hurricanes damage the Gulf Coast settlement
narrative_hooks: A free woman of color in Mobile navigates the Code Noir's restrictions while building a life in the small colonial town
A French soldier at Fort Toulouse among the Upper Creek wonders whether his distant outpost will ever amount to anything
A Creek trader arrives at Mobile with a hundred deerskins, knowing the French need his people more than his people need the French

key_events: French-Chickasaw Wars intensify as France attempts to secure the Mississippi River corridor (1730s–1740s)
Choctaw warriors, allied with France, attack Chickasaw villages in present-day northern Mississippi and Alabama
French outpost at Fort Tombecbe established on the Tombigbee River to counter English influence among the Choctaw (1735)
Mobile remains a small but strategic military and trading post
Enslaved African population in the Mobile district grows slowly
Creek Confederacy maintains neutrality, playing French and English against each other
1730s.economic_conditions: The economy remained stagnant by European standards. The deerskin trade was the primary commercial activity, with Mobile serving as the collection and export point. Small farms around Mobile grew corn, rice, and vegetables. The colony produced little that France wanted beyond furs. Military spending on forts and garrisons was the largest source of cash in the local economy. Enslaved labor was used on small farms and in domestic service rather than large-scale plantation agriculture.
1730s.migration_patterns: Very little European immigration occurred in this decade. The French population grew primarily through natural increase. Small numbers of enslaved Africans continued to arrive. The interior remained under Native sovereignty, with the Creek Confederacy controlling most of present-day Alabama. French influence extended only along rivers and at scattered military posts. The Chickasaw-French wars disrupted trade routes through northern Alabama.
1730s.social_context: The French-Chickasaw Wars created a violent frontier in the interior while Mobile remained relatively peaceful. The Creek Confederacy's skilled diplomacy — playing European powers against each other — kept them independent and powerful. In Mobile, a small but stable multiracial society continued to develop, with French, African, and Native cultural elements blending in cuisine, language, and daily practice. The Catholic Church organized community life. Free people of color held a recognized, if subordinate, social position.
1730s.daily_life_snapshot: Life in Mobile was slow-paced and subtropical. Residents tended gardens, fished in the bay, and waited for trade canoes from the interior. French soldiers at frontier posts like Fort Tombecbe lived isolated lives among Native peoples, often adopting Native dress and customs. Enslaved people in Mobile worked alongside their owners on small farms or served in households. The arrival of a ship from France or the Caribbean was a major event, bringing news, goods, and sometimes new settlers.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: French-Chickasaw Wars bring violence to the interior (1730s)
Hurricane damage to Mobile and Gulf Coast (periodic throughout 1730s)
Continued endemic disease — malaria, yellow fever, dysentery
French supply failures leave colonists and garrison underfed
narrative_hooks: A Choctaw warrior prepares for a raid on a Chickasaw village, fighting France's war as much as his own
A French commandant at Fort Tombecbe on the Tombigbee River writes a desperate letter to Mobile requesting supplies that may never come
An enslaved man in Mobile hears that the Code Noir grants him Sundays off — but his owner has other ideas

key_events: King George's War (War of Austrian Succession, 1744–1748) heightens French-English tensions in the Gulf South
French-Chickasaw conflict continues, draining French resources and manpower
Choctaw Civil War (1746–1750): pro-French and pro-English factions within the Choctaw nation clash violently
Fort Toulouse remains the key French outpost in the Creek interior
Mobile's enslaved population grows as French planters establish small-scale agriculture along Mobile Bay
English traders from Carolina and Georgia increase penetration into Creek territory, undermining French alliances
1740s.economic_conditions: The Gulf Coast economy remained minimal by European standards. The deerskin trade continued as the primary commerce, but English traders offered better goods at lower prices, threatening French trade relationships with Native nations. Small-scale agriculture around Mobile produced rice, corn, indigo, and vegetables. Military expenditures constituted a significant portion of the local economy. The colony remained a net drain on French finances.
1740s.migration_patterns: European immigration remained minimal. The French population in the Mobile district was probably under 1,000 including soldiers. Enslaved Africans constituted a growing share of the population. Interior Alabama remained under the control of the Creek Confederacy, the Choctaw, and the Chickasaw. English traders from Carolina established themselves in Creek towns, laying groundwork for future British claims. The Choctaw Civil War disrupted established trade and alliance patterns.
1740s.social_context: The Choctaw Civil War revealed the deep pressures that European colonial rivalry placed on Native societies. Within the French colonial zone around Mobile, the multiracial society continued to develop — French, African, and Native peoples interacted daily. Free people of color (gens de couleur libres) formed a small but recognized community. The Catholic Church remained the center of community life. Gender roles followed French colonial patterns, with women managing households and some owning property.
1740s.daily_life_snapshot: In Mobile, daily life followed a subtropical rhythm: early morning work, midday rest during the worst heat, evening socializing. People ate rice, corn, fish, oysters, wild game, and tropical fruits. Houses had wide porches and raised floors to cope with heat and flooding. Enslaved people worked in households, gardens, and on small farms. At frontier posts like Fort Toulouse, French soldiers lived among the Creek, learning their languages and adapting to their ways. News from Europe arrived months late.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: Choctaw Civil War (1746–1750) destabilizes France's most important Native alliance
Continued French-Chickasaw warfare in the interior
Hurricane season brought periodic devastation to Mobile
Endemic tropical disease — yellow fever, malaria — continued claiming lives
narrative_hooks: A Choctaw woman watches her village divide — half supporting the French, half turning to the English — as the Choctaw Civil War tears her people apart
A French officer at Fort Toulouse among the Upper Creek considers whether his distant outpost can hold against growing English influence
An enslaved African fisherman on Mobile Bay hauls in oysters, listening to sailors speak of distant wars between kings he will never see

key_events: French and Indian War (Seven Years' War) begins in North America (1754), with global consequences for Alabama's colonial future
Creek Confederacy maintains formal neutrality but individual towns align with French or English interests
French reinforce Fort Toulouse and Fort Tombecbe to maintain hold on interior trade routes
English colonists from Georgia and Carolina increase trade contacts with Creek and Chickasaw nations
Mobile's population remains small — perhaps 300–400 French colonists plus enslaved Africans
French defeat on the Plains of Abraham (1759) signals the likely end of French North America
1750s.economic_conditions: The deerskin trade remained the economic backbone, with both French and English competing fiercely for Native suppliers. The French and Indian War disrupted shipping and supply lines. Mobile's small agricultural sector produced rice, corn, and indigo. Military spending increased as France fortified its Gulf Coast positions. The colony remained economically marginal — its value was strategic rather than productive.
1750s.migration_patterns: No significant European immigration occurred. The French colonial population grew only through natural increase. Enslaved Africans continued arriving in small numbers. Interior Alabama remained Native territory — the Creek Confederacy controlled most of the land, and the Choctaw and Chickasaw held their respective territories. English traders and agents increasingly operated in Creek country, laying groundwork for the post-war British period.
1750s.social_context: The French and Indian War placed enormous pressure on Native alliances. Creek leaders faced the dilemma of choosing sides in a European conflict that would determine which empire dominated their homeland. In Mobile, the small French community prepared for potential English attack. Enslaved people in the colony faced uncertainty — a change of colonial regime might mean new owners and new laws. Free people of color in Mobile maintained their precarious position in the racial hierarchy.
1750s.daily_life_snapshot: Life in 1750s Mobile continued its languid Gulf Coast rhythm, now shadowed by war. Soldiers drilled at Fort Conde; merchants tallied deerskins; enslaved workers tended gardens and households. In the interior, Creek towns were vibrant autonomous communities with their own agriculture, trade networks, and governance. French soldiers at remote posts like Fort Toulouse lived lives closer to Creek culture than to Parisian norms. The war was a distant rumble until its consequences arrived.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: French and Indian War disrupts supply lines and heightens military tension (1754–1763)
Continued endemic disease in the Gulf lowlands
Uncertainty about the colony's future as French power wanes in North America
Hurricane damage to Mobile and coastal settlements
narrative_hooks: A Creek headman listens to both French and English emissaries, knowing his people's survival depends on choosing wisely — or not choosing at all
A French soldier at Fort Conde hears rumors that France may lose Canada and wonders what that means for his small world on the Gulf
An enslaved woman in Mobile prays that the distant war will not upend the fragile stability of her family

key_events: Treaty of Paris (1763): France cedes all territory east of the Mississippi to Britain; Mobile becomes British
British troops occupy Fort Conde (renamed Fort Charlotte) and assume control of Mobile (1763)
British West Florida colony established with Pensacola as capital; Mobile is the western district
Proclamation of 1763 attempts to reserve lands west of the Appalachians for Native peoples — largely ignored
English, Scottish, and Scots-Irish settlers begin arriving in the Mobile and Tombigbee districts
Creek and Choctaw nations negotiate new trade relationships with British agents
1760s.economic_conditions: The transition from French to British rule brought new traders, new goods, and new economic energy. British merchants established operations in Mobile, expanding the deerskin trade and introducing more manufactured goods. Land grants attracted English-speaking settlers for the first time. Agriculture expanded modestly with new settlers clearing land along the Tombigbee and Mobile rivers. The slave trade increased under British rule, as planters from the older English colonies saw opportunities in the fertile Gulf lowlands.
1760s.migration_patterns: The British takeover brought the first significant English-speaking migration to Alabama. Settlers from Georgia, the Carolinas, and Virginia moved into the Mobile and Tombigbee river valleys. Some brought enslaved workers to establish plantations. The existing French Creole population largely remained, adapting to British rule. Loyalists and land speculators sought grants in the new colony. Native nations continued to hold the vast interior, but English encroachment accelerated.
1760s.social_context: British rule transformed Mobile's social landscape. The French Creole community, including free people of color, suddenly found themselves under English common law rather than French civil law — a significant change that affected property rights, racial categories, and religious practice. The British Protestant establishment replaced Catholic primacy. Relations with Native nations continued through trade but with a more aggressive stance toward land acquisition. Enslaved people now fell under harsher English slave codes.
1760s.daily_life_snapshot: Mobile in the 1760s was a small town adjusting to new rulers. French Creoles continued their daily routines but now dealt with British officials, English-speaking merchants, and Protestant missionaries. British soldiers garrisoned Fort Charlotte. New settlers from the English colonies arrived with different customs, crops, and attitudes toward Native peoples and enslaved Africans. The deerskin trade still brought Creek and Choctaw traders into town, but the cultural dynamics were shifting.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: Disruption of French-era social and legal systems under British rule (1763)
Continued hurricane damage to Gulf Coast settlements
Smallpox outbreaks among Native populations facilitated by increased European contact
Friction between French Creole residents and new British administrators
narrative_hooks: A French Creole family in Mobile watches British soldiers raise the Union Jack over Fort Conde and wonders what their new world will look like
A free woman of color in Mobile discovers that British law does not recognize the same rights she held under the Code Noir
A Carolina planter arrives in the Tombigbee country with enslaved workers, seeing rich bottomland and imagining a new plantation empire

key_events: American Revolution begins (1775); West Florida remains loyal to the British Crown
Spanish Governor Bernardo de Galvez launches military campaign against British West Florida (1779–1780)
Galvez captures Fort Charlotte in Mobile after a siege (March 14, 1780)
Alabama territory passes from British to Spanish control
Creek Nation navigates the Revolution by maintaining trade with all sides
Alexander McGillivray, a Creek-Scottish leader, emerges as a dominant figure in Creek politics
1770s.economic_conditions: The American Revolution disrupted trade throughout the Gulf region. British West Florida had experienced modest economic growth in the 1770s before the war — livestock, timber, indigo, and the deerskin trade all expanded. The Spanish capture of Mobile in 1780 brought yet another change of colonial administration and trade regulations. The interior Creek economy remained largely self-sufficient, based on agriculture, hunting, and trade with European powers.
1770s.migration_patterns: American Revolution-era migration brought Loyalist refugees to West Florida from the rebellious colonies. Some settled along the Tombigbee and Tensaw rivers, bringing enslaved workers and plantation ambitions. The Spanish capture of Mobile created uncertainty but did not cause mass exodus — most residents adapted. Interior Alabama remained Creek, Choctaw, and Chickasaw territory. Some American frontier families began infiltrating the northern edges of present-day Alabama from Tennessee and Georgia.
1770s.social_context: The American Revolution made the Gulf Coast a contested borderland where British, Spanish, American, and Native interests collided. Alexander McGillivray, son of a Scottish trader and a Creek-French woman, became the most powerful Creek leader, skillfully playing European powers against each other. Mobile's population remained multiethnic — French Creoles, British settlers, Spanish soldiers, free people of color, enslaved Africans, and Native traders all mixed in the small town. Religion shifted again from British Protestantism to Spanish Catholicism.
1770s.daily_life_snapshot: Common residents of Mobile experienced the American Revolution primarily as a change of flags — from British to Spanish in 1780. Daily routines of fishing, farming, and trade continued regardless of which empire claimed sovereignty. Frontier settlers along the Tombigbee lived more isolated lives, clearing land, raising livestock, and trading with nearby Choctaw towns. Enslaved people worked in households and small farms, their legal status shifting with each change of colonial regime.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: Siege of Fort Charlotte by Spanish forces (March 1780) disrupts Mobile
American Revolution disrupts established trade networks
Uncertainty of colonial regime change — British to Spanish
Continued hurricane exposure and endemic tropical disease
narrative_hooks: A British Loyalist family fleeing the Carolina upcountry arrives on the Tombigbee River, hoping the king's territory will shelter them
Alexander McGillivray, educated in Charleston but born of Creek royalty, returns to lead his mother's people through the chaos of revolution
A Spanish soldier raises the flag over Fort Charlotte in 1780 — the third nation to claim this small town in twenty years

key_events: Treaty of Paris (1783) ends the American Revolution; Spain retains control of Mobile and West Florida
United States claims territory north of the 31st parallel, but Spain disputes the boundary
Alexander McGillivray leads the Creek Confederacy in resisting American encroachment from Georgia
Treaty of Pensacola (1784): McGillivray secures Spanish alliance and trade agreement for the Creeks
American settlers from Georgia and the Carolinas push into Creek hunting grounds, provoking conflict
Treaty of New York (1790): McGillivray negotiates directly with President Washington, ceding some Creek lands
1780s.economic_conditions: Spanish Alabama was a frontier backwater. The deerskin trade remained important but was complicated by competing American, Spanish, and British (from Canada) interests. Small-scale agriculture along the Mobile, Tensaw, and Tombigbee rivers expanded as more settlers arrived. Cotton was not yet significant. The Creek economy combined agriculture (corn, beans, squash), hunting, and trade. Land was the most valuable commodity — and the primary source of conflict between Native nations and the expanding United States.
1780s.migration_patterns: American settlers from Georgia, the Carolinas, Virginia, and Tennessee increasingly migrated toward the rich lands of the Alabama interior, despite Creek resistance. Loyalist families who had settled during the Revolution remained along the Tombigbee and Tensaw. Spanish administrators encouraged Catholic immigration. The Tensaw-Mobile delta area developed a distinctive mixed community of French Creoles, British settlers, Spanish officials, Americans, free people of color, enslaved Africans, and mixed Creek-European families.
1780s.social_context: The 1780s were defined by the struggle between American expansion and Creek sovereignty. Alexander McGillivray was the pivotal figure — a man of Creek, Scottish, and French heritage who used diplomacy to protect Creek lands. The Tensaw settlement became a unique multiethnic community. Spanish rule in Mobile was tolerant of religious diversity and existing social arrangements. Free people of color maintained property and status under Spanish law. Enslaved people worked in households and on small farms.
1780s.daily_life_snapshot: In the Tensaw-Mobile area, daily life reflected the mixed culture: French Creole cooking, English farming methods, Creek trading practices, and Spanish administrative forms coexisted. Frontier families cleared land, raised livestock, and worried about Creek raids. In Mobile, a small-town rhythm of trade, fishing, and military garrison life continued. Enslaved people grew food, tended livestock, and served in households. The wilderness was close — bears, wolves, and panthers were common, and the forest pressed in on every clearing.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: Creek-American border conflicts escalate as Georgia settlers encroach on Creek territory (1780s)
Spanish-American boundary dispute creates legal uncertainty for settlers
Periodic hurricanes and flooding in the Gulf lowlands
Malaria and yellow fever remain endemic
narrative_hooks: Alexander McGillivray rides to New York in 1790 to negotiate face-to-face with George Washington — a Creek leader meeting the American president as an equal
A Tensaw settler's family includes French, Creek, and African ancestry — in their household, the boundaries between races are lived differently than in Virginia or Georgia
An American squatter on Creek land watches nervously as Creek warriors pass on the trail, knowing he has no legal right to be there

key_events: Death of Alexander McGillivray (February 17, 1793) weakens Creek political unity
Treaty of San Lorenzo (Pinckney's Treaty, 1795): Spain recognizes 31st parallel as boundary, opening the way for American expansion
Eli Whitney's cotton gin patented (1794), setting the stage for cotton's explosive growth in Alabama
Mississippi Territory created by Congress (1798), including future Alabama
American settlers accelerate migration into the Tombigbee and Tensaw districts
Creek Nation faces increasing internal divisions between accommodationists and traditionalists
1790s.economic_conditions: The cotton gin transformed the economic calculus of the Gulf South. Short-staple cotton could now be profitably processed, making the rich soils of Alabama's Black Belt and river valleys enormously valuable. The transition from deerskin trade to cotton agriculture began. American settlers brought enslaved workers and plantation ambitions. Land speculation intensified. The old colonial economy of Mobile — fur trade, subsistence farming, military garrison — was about to be overwhelmed by the cotton frontier.
1790s.migration_patterns: The 1790s saw a dramatic acceleration of American migration into what would become Alabama. Settlers from Georgia, the Carolinas, Virginia, Tennessee, and Kentucky poured into the Tombigbee and Tensaw districts. Many brought enslaved workers. The creation of Mississippi Territory (1798) gave federal administrative structure to the region. Creek, Choctaw, and Chickasaw nations still held vast territories, but pressure on their lands intensified with every wagon train of settlers.
1790s.social_context: The death of McGillivray removed the most skillful Creek diplomatic leader, leaving the confederacy more vulnerable to American pressure. Within Creek society, a divide deepened between leaders who favored accommodation with Americans and traditionalists who rejected land cessions. In the Tensaw-Mobile district, American newcomers brought a harder racial hierarchy than the old French-Spanish-Creek hybrid culture. Enslaved people were arriving in larger numbers as the cotton economy took hold. The era of multiracial frontier fluidity was ending.
1790s.daily_life_snapshot: Frontier settlers in the 1790s lived in log cabins, cleared land with axes and fire, planted corn and cotton, and raised hogs that foraged in the woods. Diet was corn in every form — pone, hominy, mush — plus salt pork, wild game, and garden vegetables. Households were largely self-sufficient: women spun, wove, and sewed clothing; men hunted, farmed, and built. In Mobile, a somewhat more cosmopolitan life continued, with merchants, soldiers, and traders mixing in the small port town.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: Death of McGillivray (1793) destabilizes Creek diplomacy and unity
Increasing Creek-American frontier violence as settlers encroach on Native lands
Yellow fever epidemic in the Gulf region (1790s)
Contested sovereignty creates legal chaos — Spanish, American, and Creek claims overlap
narrative_hooks: A Creek elder mourns McGillivray's death and wonders who will hold the nation together against the American tide
A Georgia planter loads his family and enslaved workers onto wagons, heading for the rich land of the Alabama frontier — and the cotton fortune he imagines there
A free Black family in the Tensaw settlement watches American laws replace Spanish ones, knowing their rights may shrink with the change

key_events: Mississippi Territory expanded; Washington, Mississippi Territory, serves as capital
American settlers flood into the Tombigbee and Tensaw districts, establishing cotton plantations
Fort Stoddert established near Mobile as the principal American military post (1799, expanded 1800s)
Aaron Burr's conspiracy allegedly targets the Gulf South, including Alabama territory (1806–1807)
Tension between Americans and remaining Spanish authority in Mobile district
Creek Nation under increasing pressure as the U.S. demands land cessions through treaties
1800s.economic_conditions: Cotton agriculture exploded in the early 1800s, transforming the Alabama frontier from a fur-trading backwater into an agricultural bonanza. Settlers with enslaved labor forces cleared Black Belt prairies and river bottoms for cotton. The price of cotton on world markets incentivized rapid expansion. Land speculation became rampant. The old deerskin trade collapsed as Native hunting grounds were converted to cotton fields. Mobile remained under disputed Spanish control, complicating trade and exports.
1800s.migration_patterns: The great Alabama land rush began. Settlers from Virginia, the Carolinas, Georgia, Tennessee, and Kentucky arrived in large numbers, many with enslaved workers. The richest brought dozens of enslaved people to establish large plantations immediately. Poorer settlers claimed smaller tracts and aspired to join the planter class. Creek, Choctaw, and Chickasaw lands were targeted for cession. The Tensaw settlement grew rapidly. This was the opening wave of what would become Alabama Fever — the frenzied rush for cotton land.
1800s.social_context: American social norms replaced the more fluid French-Spanish colonial culture. Racial categories hardened: slavery became more brutal and codified, free people of color lost rights, and Native peoples were treated as obstacles to be removed. The evangelical Protestant denominations — Baptist and Methodist — arrived with the settlers, challenging the old Catholic establishment in Mobile. A raw frontier democracy coexisted with extreme inequality between slaveholders and the enslaved.
1800s.daily_life_snapshot: Frontier Alabama in the 1800s was a land of log cabins, stumpy clearings, and muddy trails. Settlers ate corn, pork, and whatever they could hunt or grow. Malaria was endemic in the river bottoms. Men cleared land from dawn to dark; women managed households, children, gardens, and livestock. Enslaved people did the hardest labor — felling trees, grubbing stumps, and plowing virgin soil for cotton. Social life centered on camp meetings, militia musters, and court days.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: Malaria and other fevers endemic in newly cleared river bottoms
Frontier violence between settlers and Creek warriors
Political uncertainty from overlapping Spanish and American claims
Isolation — roads barely existed; rivers were the only reliable transportation
narrative_hooks: A Virginia planter arrives in the Tombigbee valley with twenty enslaved people, seeing in the Black Belt soil the cotton fortune that exhausted Virginia land could no longer provide
An enslaved family is marched overland from Georgia to the Alabama frontier, forced to build a new plantation from raw wilderness
A Creek woman watches American surveyors mapping her people's land and understands what the lines they draw will mean

key_events: U.S. forces seize Mobile from Spain (April 15, 1813), claiming it as part of the Louisiana Purchase
Creek War erupts: Red Stick faction attacks Fort Mims, killing approximately 250 settlers and mixed-Creek families (August 30, 1813)
Andrew Jackson leads Tennessee militia, allied Cherokee, Choctaw, and Lower Creek forces against the Red Sticks
Battle of Horseshoe Bend (March 27, 1814): Jackson's forces destroy Red Stick resistance
Treaty of Fort Jackson (August 9, 1814): Creek Nation forced to cede approximately 23 million acres — half of present-day Alabama
Alabama Territory created (March 3, 1817), separated from Mississippi Territory
Alabama admitted as the 22nd state (December 14, 1819); Huntsville serves as first capital
1810s.economic_conditions: The Creek War and Treaty of Fort Jackson opened vast lands for cotton cultivation. 'Alabama Fever' swept the South — land-hungry planters and speculators rushed in. Cotton prices were high, and the rich Black Belt soil produced extraordinary yields. Land office sales in Huntsville broke national records. The acquisition of Mobile gave Alabama a Gulf port for cotton exports. By 1819, the cotton boom was in full swing, though the Panic of 1819 briefly cooled land speculation.
1810s.migration_patterns: The 1810s saw the most dramatic migration wave in Alabama history. After the Creek cession, tens of thousands of settlers poured in from Virginia, the Carolinas, Georgia, Tennessee, and Kentucky. Many brought enslaved workers — the forced migration of enslaved people to Alabama was massive. The richest planters claimed Black Belt prairie land; small farmers took hill country. Creek survivors were confined to a fraction of their former territory. The Alabama frontier was transformed from Native homeland to cotton kingdom in less than a decade.
1810s.social_context: The Creek War was catastrophic for Native peoples and transformative for the American South. The Red Stick resistance was a cultural and military response to American encroachment — and its defeat opened the floodgates. Andrew Jackson became a national hero. Alabama society formed rapidly along Southern plantation lines: a planter elite at the top, yeoman farmers in the middle, poor whites below, and enslaved African Americans at the bottom doing the labor that made everything possible. The first state constitution (1819) established slavery and limited political rights to white men.
1810s.daily_life_snapshot: The 1810s in Alabama were chaotic and violent. Before 1814, frontier families lived in fear of Creek raids — the Fort Mims massacre haunted the territory. After Horseshoe Bend, the mood shifted to frenzied land grabbing. Settlers cleared land at breakneck speed, built crude cabins, and planted cotton. Enslaved people did the heaviest work — felling forest, breaking ground, and planting in soil still tangled with roots. Roads were terrible; rivers were the highways. Malaria, dysentery, and snakebite were constant dangers.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: Fort Mims massacre (August 30, 1813) kills approximately 250 settlers and mixed-Creek families
Creek War devastates both Native and settler communities (1813–1814)
Panic of 1819 disrupts land sales and cotton prices
Malaria epidemics in newly cleared river bottomlands
Forced march of enslaved people from older states to Alabama frontier
narrative_hooks: A mixed-Creek family shelters inside Fort Mims on August 30, 1813, as Red Stick warriors breach the gate — and the world they knew ends
Andrew Jackson rides south with Tennessee volunteers after Fort Mims, driven by fury and ambition in equal measure
An enslaved man who helped clear the first cotton field on a new Black Belt plantation watches the white bolls open and understands that this crop will define his life and his children's lives
A Creek mother carries her child away from Horseshoe Bend, walking toward an uncertain future on a fraction of her people's homeland

key_events: Alabama's capital moves from Huntsville to Cahaba (1820), then to Tuscaloosa (1826)
Cotton production expands dramatically across the Black Belt and Tennessee Valley
Missouri Compromise (1820) heightens sectional tensions over slavery — Alabama is firmly proslavery
Federal land offices in Huntsville, Cahaba, and St. Stephens sell millions of acres
Enslaved population grows rapidly through forced migration from Upper South states
Creek and Choctaw nations face continued pressure for land cessions
1820s.economic_conditions: Alabama in the 1820s was a cotton boom state. The Black Belt — named for its dark, fertile prairie soil — became one of the most productive cotton regions in the world. Planters accumulated vast wealth measured in land and enslaved people. Cotton prices fluctuated but remained profitable enough to fuel expansion. Small farmers in the hill country raised corn, livestock, and modest amounts of cotton. Mobile grew as the primary export port, with cotton bales stacked on its wharves.
1820s.migration_patterns: The flood of migration continued. Planters from Virginia, the Carolinas, and Georgia relocated entire plantation operations — enslaved workers, livestock, and tools — to Alabama's Black Belt. Yeoman farmers from Tennessee, Kentucky, and the Carolinas settled the hill country. The forced migration of enslaved people was staggering: Alabama's enslaved population roughly tripled during the 1820s. Creek and Choctaw peoples clung to remaining lands under enormous pressure.
1820s.social_context: Alabama society crystallized into the Southern plantation pattern. The planter class — those owning 20 or more enslaved people — dominated politics, economics, and social life despite being a minority of the white population. The majority of whites were small farmers who owned few or no enslaved people but aspired to planter status and supported the slave system. Enslaved African Americans built the cotton economy with their labor under conditions of extreme exploitation. Religion — primarily Baptist and Methodist — provided community structure for whites and, in separate and controlled settings, for enslaved people.
1820s.daily_life_snapshot: On Black Belt plantations, enslaved workers rose before dawn, labored in cotton fields until dark, and returned to cramped cabins. Planters lived in increasingly grand houses and cultivated genteel Southern culture. Yeoman farmers in the hill country lived more modestly — log houses, small fields, subsistence crops supplemented by hunting. Mobile developed a cosmopolitan Gulf Coast culture with its French Creole heritage, theater, and commerce. Steamboats began appearing on Alabama's rivers, transforming trade and travel.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: Cotton price fluctuations cause periodic financial stress for planters and merchants
Cahaba, the first permanent capital, proves flood-prone and unhealthy
Forced march of thousands of enslaved people to Alabama from Upper South states
Continued malaria and disease in river bottomlands
narrative_hooks: An enslaved man sold from a Virginia tobacco farm walks in a coffle to the Alabama Black Belt, leaving behind everyone he has ever known
A planter's wife arrives at her new Alabama plantation to find a half-built house surrounded by stumps and mud — and must create a household from nothing
A Choctaw leader signs a treaty ceding land he knows his people need, under pressure he cannot resist
A hill country farmer watches his Black Belt neighbor grow rich on cotton and wonders whether he, too, should buy an enslaved worker

key_events: Indian Removal Act signed by President Andrew Jackson (May 28, 1830)
Treaty of Dancing Rabbit Creek (1830): Choctaw cede remaining lands in Mississippi and Alabama — removal west follows
Treaty of Cusseta (1832): Creek Nation forced to cede all remaining Alabama lands; removal to Indian Territory (Oklahoma) begins
Second Creek War (1836): starving Creek resistance leads to forced military removal
Chickasaw cede lands in northern Alabama (1832); removal follows
Tuscaloosa serves as state capital (1826–1846)
Alabama's cotton production makes it one of the wealthiest states in the Union
1830s.economic_conditions: The 1830s were the peak of Alabama Fever. With Native removal opening millions of additional acres, cotton production soared. Alabama became the leading cotton-producing state in the nation. Planters grew fabulously wealthy; the Black Belt was one of the richest agricultural regions in the world. Land prices boomed, then crashed in the Panic of 1837. Speculation was rampant — fortunes were made and lost. Enslaved labor drove the entire system.
1830s.migration_patterns: Indian removal was the defining migration event. The Creek, Choctaw, and Chickasaw were forcibly removed from Alabama to Indian Territory (Oklahoma) in marches that killed thousands — Alabama's Trail of Tears. Simultaneously, white settlers and enslaved workers poured onto the vacated lands. Alabama's population nearly doubled in the 1830s. The enslaved population grew even faster, driven by the domestic slave trade and planter migration from the Upper South.
1830s.social_context: Indian removal was a moral catastrophe carried out with bipartisan political support. Alabama's white citizens overwhelmingly supported removal and rushed to occupy Native lands. The planter class consolidated political and economic power. The Panic of 1837 temporarily disrupted the boom but did not change the fundamental social order. Enslaved people lived under increasingly harsh conditions as the plantation system matured. Yeoman farmers in the hill country participated in the cotton economy at smaller scale but were politically marginalized by the planter elite.
1830s.daily_life_snapshot: For enslaved people, the 1830s meant unrelenting cotton labor — plowing, planting, chopping, and picking from spring to winter. Plantation owners built columned houses and emulated the Virginia aristocracy. Hill country farmers lived simpler lives, raising corn, pigs, and some cotton. Mobile was a booming port city during cotton shipping season, with bales stacked stories high on the waterfront. The arrival of steamboats on the Alabama, Tombigbee, and Tennessee rivers connected isolated communities to markets.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: Forced removal of Creek, Choctaw, and Chickasaw peoples — thousands die on the march (1830s)
Second Creek War (1836): starving Creek people resist removal and are forcibly marched west
Panic of 1837 collapses land values and cotton prices
Continued forced migration of enslaved people through the domestic slave trade
Malaria and cholera in river communities
narrative_hooks: A Creek mother wraps her child in a blanket and begins the march west to Indian Territory, leaving the homeland her ancestors knew for centuries
A slave trader marches a coffle of fifty enslaved people from Virginia through the Alabama hill country to the Black Belt auction block
A Tuscaloosa land speculator watches cotton prices soar in 1836 and borrows heavily — then watches everything collapse in the Panic of 1837
An enslaved man on a Black Belt plantation picks cotton until his fingers bleed, knowing every bale makes his owner richer and his own freedom more distant

key_events: Montgomery becomes the state capital (1846), reflecting the political power of the Black Belt
Alabama's cotton production continues to expand, making it a dominant force in the global cotton market
Mexican-American War (1846–1848): Alabama sends volunteers; the war opens new territory and intensifies the slavery debate
William Lowndes Yancey emerges as Alabama's leading voice for Southern rights and eventual secession
Second Great Awakening continues to shape religious life — Baptist and Methodist churches dominate
Alabama's enslaved population exceeds 340,000 by 1850
1840s.economic_conditions: Cotton was king. Alabama produced more cotton than any other state for much of the 1840s. The Black Belt and Tennessee Valley were the most productive regions. Planters reinvested profits in land and enslaved people, creating a self-reinforcing cycle of expansion. Mobile was the second-largest cotton port in the nation after New Orleans. Recovery from the Panic of 1837 was gradual but complete by mid-decade. Yeoman farmers in the hill country grew corn and raised livestock, participating in the cotton economy at the margins.
1840s.migration_patterns: Migration into Alabama continued, though at a slower pace than the 1830s boom. The domestic slave trade continued delivering enslaved people from Virginia, Maryland, and the Carolinas. Some Alabamians began moving further west — to Mississippi, Louisiana, Texas, and Arkansas — seeking fresh cotton land as Alabama soils showed early signs of exhaustion in the oldest-planted areas. Mobile attracted immigrants from Ireland and Germany who worked as laborers and dockworkers.
1840s.social_context: Antebellum Alabama society reached its mature form. The planter aristocracy — centered in the Black Belt and the Tennessee Valley — controlled state politics and culture. William Lowndes Yancey voiced the increasingly radical proslavery position. Yeoman farmers in the hill country and wiregrass region had different economic interests but generally supported the slave system. Enslaved people built community through family networks, religious gatherings, and shared labor. Mobile's cosmopolitan culture — with its Mardi Gras, theater, and diverse population — stood apart from the rural plantation world.
1840s.daily_life_snapshot: On the great Black Belt plantations, a rigid schedule governed enslaved workers: horn at dawn, fieldwork until dark, corn and pork rations, and quarters that ranged from drafty cabins to semi-organized 'streets' behind the big house. Planters cultivated gentility — books, music, and visiting. Hill country families lived simpler, harder lives: log homes, subsistence farming, occasional cash from cotton or livestock sales. In Mobile, dockworkers loaded cotton bales onto ships bound for Liverpool, New York, and New England mills.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: Soil exhaustion in the oldest cotton-growing areas forces some planters to move west
Continued forced separation of enslaved families through the domestic slave trade
Yellow fever and cholera outbreaks in Mobile and river towns
Mexican-American War casualties among Alabama volunteers
narrative_hooks: An enslaved woman on a Black Belt plantation hears that her husband has been sold to a trader heading for Texas — she will never see him again
A hill country farmer drives his hogs to market in Montgomery, passing through the Black Belt's columned plantation houses that represent a world he cannot enter
An Irish immigrant unloads cotton bales on the Mobile waterfront, earning wages that would have been unimaginable in starving County Cork
William Lowndes Yancey thunders about Southern rights at a political rally, and a planter in the crowd feels the first stirrings of secession

key_events: Compromise of 1850 temporarily quiets the slavery debate but satisfies neither side
Alabama's fire-eaters, led by William Lowndes Yancey, push for Southern secession
Kansas-Nebraska Act (1854) and 'Bleeding Kansas' inflame sectional passions
John Brown's raid on Harpers Ferry (October 1859) terrifies Alabama slaveholders
Alabama's enslaved population reaches approximately 435,000 — nearly half the total population
Mobile and Selma Railroad and other rail lines expand, connecting cotton regions to ports
Democratic Party splits along sectional lines; Yancey walks out of the 1860 Charleston convention
1850s.economic_conditions: The 1850s were the apex of Alabama's antebellum cotton economy. Prices were high, production was enormous, and the planter class reached peak wealth. The value of enslaved people — Alabama's largest 'capital asset' — soared. Railroad construction connected interior plantations to Mobile and other markets. Some economic diversification began: iron production in the Jones Valley (future Birmingham area), small manufacturing in towns. But cotton dominated everything. The average Black Belt planter was among the wealthiest people in America.
1850s.migration_patterns: Migration into Alabama slowed as the best cotton lands were taken. The domestic slave trade continued, with enslaved people forced into Alabama from the Upper South. Some Alabama planters moved operations to fresh lands in Texas, Louisiana, and Arkansas. Within Alabama, the hill country and wiregrass regions grew as yeoman farmers expanded subsistence operations. Mobile continued attracting European immigrants — Irish and German workers especially. Free Black communities existed in Mobile and a few other towns under severe legal restrictions.
1850s.social_context: The slavery debate consumed Alabama politics. Fire-eaters like Yancey demanded aggressive defense of slavery and threatened secession. The planter class saw any restriction on slavery as an existential threat. Non-slaveholding whites were politically divided: many supported the planter class out of racial solidarity, but hill country farmers in north Alabama harbored Unionist sentiment. Enslaved people heard whispers of abolitionism and John Brown and maintained hope, however dangerous. Mobile's free Black community — though small — maintained churches, schools, and businesses under constant legal threat.
1850s.daily_life_snapshot: The 1850s saw antebellum Alabama at its most refined and most brutal. Grand plantation houses hosted balls and barbecues. Enslaved people labored from can-see to can't-see in the cotton fields. Town life in Montgomery, Mobile, Selma, and Tuscaloosa offered theaters, churches, and commerce. Railroads began replacing steamboats for some routes. In the hill country, life remained simpler — subsistence farming, hunting, and occasional trips to the county seat. The political atmosphere was electric with talk of secession and war.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: Intensifying sectional crisis creates fear and uncertainty throughout the state
Continued brutal exploitation of enslaved workers on cotton plantations
Yellow fever epidemics in Mobile (1853 and other years)
Economic dependence on a single crop (cotton) creates vulnerability
Increasing restrictions on free Black communities
narrative_hooks: A planter reads news of John Brown's raid and checks the locks on the slave quarters, understanding that the world he built on human bondage may not endure
An enslaved blacksmith in Selma hears whispered talk of abolition and freedom — and knows that speaking of it aloud could cost his life
A hill country farmer in north Alabama listens to secession talk and thinks: I don't own any slaves — why should I fight a rich man's war?
A fire-eater delivers a speech in Montgomery calling for Alabama to leave the Union, and the crowd roars approval

key_events: Alabama secedes from the Union (January 11, 1861) — among the first states to leave
Confederate States of America organized in Montgomery; Jefferson Davis inaugurated as president at the Alabama State Capitol (February 18, 1861)
Montgomery serves as the first Confederate capital before it moves to Richmond (May 1861)
Battle of Mobile Bay: Admiral David Farragut's fleet defeats Confederate forces ('Damn the torpedoes, full speed ahead!') (August 5, 1864)
Battle of Selma: Union cavalry under James H. Wilson destroys the Confederacy's last major arsenal (April 2, 1865)
Wilson's Raid through Alabama devastates Confederate infrastructure (March–April 1865)
Emancipation: approximately 435,000 enslaved Alabamians gain freedom (1865)
Reconstruction begins; Alabama readmitted to the Union (1868) under a new constitution granting Black suffrage
1860s.economic_conditions: The Civil War destroyed Alabama's antebellum economy. Confederate currency became worthless; plantation infrastructure was damaged or destroyed; the enslaved labor force that underpinned the cotton economy was freed. Wilson's Raid destroyed Selma's ironworks, foundries, and arsenal. Mobile was blockaded and its commerce strangled. Reconstruction brought economic chaos: former planters lacked labor; formerly enslaved people lacked land, tools, and capital. Sharecropping and tenant farming emerged as the new — and deeply exploitative — labor system.
dominant_industries: cotton cultivation (disrupted by war, slowly resuming under sharecropping)
iron and arms manufacturing (destroyed by Wilson's Raid)
1860s.migration_patterns: The Civil War displaced thousands — refugees fled battlefields, Union cavalry raids, and economic collapse. After emancipation, formerly enslaved people moved to seek family members separated by the slave trade, to find work, and to build new lives. Some freedpeople established independent communities; others remained on or near former plantations as sharecroppers. White Alabamians in the hill country who had opposed secession faced violence from Confederate authorities during the war. Some escaped to Union lines.
1860s.social_context: The 1860s transformed Alabama more than any other decade. Secession and the Confederacy were driven by the planter class's determination to preserve slavery. The war killed approximately 35,000 Alabama soldiers. Emancipation overturned the social order. During Reconstruction, Black Alabamians voted, held office, and built schools and churches for the first time. White supremacist violence — including early Ku Klux Klan activity — sought to reverse these gains. The fundamental question of the decade: would freedom be real or merely nominal?
1860s.daily_life_snapshot: During the war, Alabama civilians faced shortages of food, clothing, medicine, and salt. Women ran farms and plantations alone while men fought. Enslaved people did the work that kept the Confederacy functioning — and many fled to Union lines when possible. After the war, chaos: freedpeople searched for family members, negotiated labor contracts, and built churches and schools. Former planters tried to recreate the plantation system through sharecropping. Hunger, disease, and violence were widespread.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: Civil War casualties: approximately 35,000 Alabama soldiers killed
Wilson's Raid destroys Selma, Tuscaloosa, and other towns (April 1865)
Union naval blockade strangles Mobile's commerce (1861–1865)
Widespread starvation and disease during and after the war
Ku Klux Klan and white supremacist violence against freedpeople and Republicans (late 1860s)
narrative_hooks: Jefferson Davis takes the oath of office on the portico of the Alabama State Capitol in Montgomery, launching a nation built on slavery
An enslaved man on a Black Belt plantation hears that he is free — and must decide what freedom means when he owns nothing and knows no other home
A hill country Unionist hides in the woods to avoid Confederate conscription, hunted by his own neighbors
A freedwoman walks miles to a Freedmen's Bureau office to register the name she has chosen for herself — the first time her name has ever appeared in an official record
Admiral Farragut's fleet pushes into Mobile Bay under Confederate fire in August 1864

key_events: Reconstruction continues in Alabama; Black legislators serve in the state legislature
Alabama's 1868 constitution grants universal male suffrage and establishes a public school system
Ku Klux Klan and other white supremacist groups terrorize Black voters and Republican officeholders
Democrats ('Redeemers') recapture the Alabama state government (1874), ending Reconstruction
Birmingham founded (1871) at the crossing of two railroad lines, near iron ore, coal, and limestone deposits
Convict leasing system begins: the state leases prisoners — disproportionately Black men — to private companies for forced labor
1870s.economic_conditions: Reconstruction Alabama was economically devastated. The cotton economy resumed under the sharecropping system, which trapped both Black and white tenant farmers in cycles of debt. The founding of Birmingham in 1871 signaled the beginning of Alabama's industrial economy — iron, coal, and steel. But the Redeemer government prioritized low taxes and cheap labor over investment. The convict leasing system provided forced labor to mines and railroads at enormous human cost. Cotton prices declined through the decade.
dominant_industries: iron and coal mining (Birmingham district, emerging)
1870s.migration_patterns: Formerly enslaved people continued to move within Alabama, seeking better sharecropping terms, reuniting with family, and establishing independent communities. Some Black Alabamians began migrating to cities — Montgomery, Mobile, Birmingham. White migration into the Birmingham district began as iron and coal prospects attracted investors and workers. The Redeemer takeover prompted some Black political leaders to leave the state. Hill country whites remained relatively stable in their communities.
1870s.social_context: The Redemption of 1874 was a violent counterrevolution. White Democrats used intimidation, fraud, and Klan terror to destroy Black political power. The brief experiment of multiracial democracy — Black legislators, integrated public services, public schools — was crushed. The convict leasing system became a new form of slavery, subjecting prisoners (overwhelmingly Black men arrested on trivial charges) to forced labor in mines and plantations under conditions that killed thousands. The 1870s established the framework of white supremacy that would define Alabama for nearly a century.
1870s.daily_life_snapshot: For Black Alabamians, the 1870s began with hope and ended with repression. Freedpeople built churches, schools, and families during Reconstruction — then watched as Redeemers stripped away political rights. Sharecropping meant annual cycles of planting, harvesting, and settling up at the crossroads store, where debts never seemed to shrink. For white yeoman farmers, life was also hard: low cotton prices, poor roads, and limited access to credit. Birmingham was a raw boomtown of mud streets, railroad workers, and iron furnaces.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: Ku Klux Klan terror against Black voters and Republican officials (early 1870s)
Redeemer takeover ends Black political participation (1874)
Convict leasing begins — prisoners forced into mines and labor camps under brutal conditions
Cotton price decline impoverishes sharecroppers and small farmers
Yellow fever outbreaks in Mobile and river towns
narrative_hooks: A Black state legislator casts his last vote before Redeemer Democrats force him from office through threats and fraud
A young Black man arrested for vagrancy is leased to a coal mine in the Birmingham district — entering a system designed to re-enslave him
A freedwoman teaches reading to former slaves in a one-room schoolhouse built by the Black community, knowing that white supremacists may burn it down
An investor from the North arrives in Birmingham in 1871, seeing in the red ore hills the future of Southern industry

key_events: Birmingham booms as iron and steel production expands — dubbed 'The Magic City'
Booker T. Washington arrives at Tuskegee to establish the Tuskegee Normal and Industrial Institute (1881)
Convict leasing expands; Pratt Coal and Coke Company and other firms exploit leased prisoners in mines
Railroad expansion connects Alabama's cities and industries to national markets
Colored Farmers' Alliance organizes Black agricultural workers across the Black Belt
The 'Bourbon' Democratic establishment maintains one-party rule through fraud and intimidation
1880s.economic_conditions: Birmingham's iron and steel industry grew rapidly, making it the industrial center of the New South. Cheap labor — including convict leasing — kept production costs low. Cotton remained the dominant agricultural product, but sharecropping impoverished the people who grew it. Booker T. Washington's Tuskegee Institute offered Black Alabamians vocational education as a path to economic self-sufficiency. Railroad expansion improved market access but primarily benefited industrialists and large planters. The economic system was designed to keep labor cheap and plentiful.
1880s.migration_patterns: Birmingham attracted workers from across Alabama and the South — Black and white men seeking industrial wages. The city grew from a crossroads to a major Southern city in a single decade. Rural-to-urban migration began in earnest. Some Black Alabamians participated in the Exoduster movement, migrating to Kansas. Within rural Alabama, sharecroppers moved frequently, seeking better terms. The Birmingham district drew European immigrants in small numbers — primarily from Italy, Greece, and Eastern Europe — to work in mines and foundries.
1880s.social_context: The 1880s established the New South paradox: industrial progress alongside racial oppression. Booker T. Washington's philosophy of accommodation and vocational education reflected both pragmatism and the limited options available to Black Southerners. Convict leasing was a horror: death rates in Alabama's prison mines exceeded those of many slave plantations. Birmingham's rapid growth created a diverse but rigidly segregated city. The Colored Farmers' Alliance showed that Black agricultural workers could organize, but white violence crushed most such efforts.
1880s.daily_life_snapshot: In Birmingham, foundry workers — Black and white — labored in extreme heat pouring molten iron, while miners dug coal in dangerous underground tunnels. Housing was crude: company-owned shacks near the mines. In the Black Belt, sharecroppers followed the cotton cycle: plow, plant, chop, pick, settle up. At Tuskegee, students learned trades — brickmaking, carpentry, farming — that Washington believed would lift the race. In Mobile, the old Gulf Coast culture persisted alongside New South industry. Churches — Black and white — were the center of community life.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: Convict leasing deaths in Alabama mines reach horrific levels — hundreds die annually
Sharecropping traps rural families in debt peonage
Industrial accidents in Birmingham's mines and foundries kill and maim workers
Yellow fever outbreaks continue in coastal and river towns
Political repression prevents Black citizens from exercising their rights
narrative_hooks: Booker T. Washington arrives at Tuskegee in 1881 to find a dilapidated building and boundless ambition — and begins building an institution that will shape Black America
A convict laborer in a Birmingham coal mine scratches another day's mark on the tunnel wall, not knowing if he will survive to see his sentence end
A Black sharecropper's wife keeps careful account of what the family owes at the crossroads store, knowing the numbers never add up to freedom
A Birmingham ironworker, newly arrived from a Black Belt cotton farm, feels the blast furnace heat and wonders if this is what progress feels like

key_events: Alabama Constitutional Convention of 1901 (convened in 1901 but rooted in 1890s politics) — designed explicitly to disenfranchise Black voters
Plessy v. Ferguson (1896) upholds 'separate but equal,' providing legal framework for Jim Crow
Populist movement challenges Bourbon Democratic establishment; Reuben Kolb campaigns for governor (1892, 1894) with biracial support — defeated by fraud
Lynching reaches epidemic levels — Alabama is among the deadliest states for racial violence
Birmingham steel industry grows; Tennessee Coal, Iron and Railroad Company (TCI) becomes dominant
Tuskegee Institute expands under Booker T. Washington; George Washington Carver arrives (1896)
1890s.economic_conditions: The 1890s brought economic depression nationally (Panic of 1893), hitting Alabama's farmers and industrial workers hard. Cotton prices fell to historic lows — under 5 cents a pound — devastating sharecroppers. Birmingham's iron and steel industry suffered from national depression but recovered by decade's end. The Populist movement articulated the grievances of poor farmers, both Black and white, but was defeated by the planter-industrial establishment through fraud and violence. Tuskegee Institute continued promoting Black economic self-sufficiency.
1890s.migration_patterns: Rural poverty drove some Alabamians off the land and into Birmingham and other towns. Early trickles of Black out-migration to Northern cities began, foreshadowing the Great Migration. Within Alabama, movement from countryside to Birmingham continued. Some white hill country farmers moved to textile mill towns. European immigrants continued arriving in Birmingham's industrial district in small numbers. The 1901 Constitutional Convention's explicit goal was to lock Black Alabamians in place — without political power or economic mobility.
1890s.social_context: The 1890s were a nadir for Black Alabamians. Jim Crow laws multiplied — segregating railroads, streetcars, public spaces, and every aspect of daily life. Lynching was a tool of racial terror: Alabama recorded more lynchings per capita than almost any other state. The Populist movement briefly threatened the racial order by uniting poor Black and white farmers, and the planter-industrial elite responded by making disenfranchisement and segregation the law. The 1901 Constitution would formalize what violence and fraud had already achieved.
1890s.daily_life_snapshot: For Black Alabamians, the 1890s meant grinding poverty, legal segregation, and the constant threat of racial violence. Cotton prices so low that families worked all year and ended deeper in debt. For white sharecroppers, conditions were nearly as bad economically, though without the added burden of racial terror. In Birmingham, industrial workers — Black in the most dangerous jobs, white in the better-paid ones — lived in company housing and depended on company stores. At Tuskegee, students learned skills and self-respect in defiance of the system designed to keep them down.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: Panic of 1893 devastates Alabama's economy — cotton prices collapse, mines close
Epidemic lynching — dozens killed by racial mob violence in the 1890s
Populist movement defeated through electoral fraud and intimidation
Convict leasing continues to kill hundreds in Alabama mines
Rural poverty drives families to the edge of starvation
narrative_hooks: A Black farmer and a white farmer stand together at a Populist rally in 1892, united by poverty — but the men who run Alabama will destroy that alliance
George Washington Carver arrives at Tuskegee in 1896, carrying a suitcase full of plant specimens and a vision for Southern agriculture
A Black man is lynched in a small Alabama town for a crime he did not commit — and the local newspaper reports it as a matter of public order
A delegate to the 1901 Constitutional Convention says openly that the purpose is to remove Black citizens from political life — and no one in the room objects

key_events: Alabama Constitution of 1901 ratified — explicitly designed to disenfranchise Black voters through poll taxes, literacy tests, and grandfather clauses
Black voter registration drops from approximately 180,000 to fewer than 3,000 within a decade
Montgomery streetcar boycott (1900–1902): Black residents boycott segregated streetcars — an early precursor to the civil rights movement
Birmingham's steel industry grows; U.S. Steel acquires TCI (1907)
Convict leasing continues; conditions in Pratt Mines and Banner Mine are notorious
Boll weevil begins its westward march from Mexico, reaching Alabama by the early 1910s
1900s.economic_conditions: The 1901 Constitution locked in a low-wage, low-tax economic model that benefited industrialists and large planters. Birmingham steel boomed — U.S. Steel's acquisition of TCI in 1907 made it Alabama's largest employer. But wages were kept low, and Black workers were confined to the most dangerous, lowest-paid jobs. Cotton remained the agricultural staple, but the boll weevil threat loomed. Timber companies stripped Alabama's forests for lumber. The state's economy was structured to extract wealth while keeping labor costs minimal.
1900s.migration_patterns: The 1901 Constitution and worsening racial conditions pushed Black Alabamians toward out-migration. The early trickle of the Great Migration began — Black families from the Black Belt and Birmingham headed north to Chicago, Detroit, Cleveland, and Pittsburgh. Within Alabama, rural-to-urban migration continued. Birmingham's population swelled with workers drawn to steel and coal jobs. European immigrants — Italians, Greeks, Syrians, Eastern Europeans — added to Birmingham's ethnic diversity. White hill country families began moving to textile mill towns.
1900s.social_context: The 1901 Constitution was white supremacy codified. Its drafters said openly that the purpose was to eliminate Black political power. Combined with Jim Crow segregation, convict leasing, lynching, and economic exploitation, it created a system of racial caste that would endure for over six decades. Booker T. Washington remained the most prominent Black leader, advocating accommodation while privately funding legal challenges. The Montgomery streetcar boycott (1900–1902) showed that Black resistance was alive. But the decade belonged to white supremacy.
1900s.daily_life_snapshot: For most Alabamians, life in the 1900s was hard. Black sharecroppers worked cotton under the same conditions as in the 1870s — if anything, legal protections were fewer. In Birmingham, industrial workers faced long hours, dangerous conditions, and company-controlled housing. White farmers in the hill country and wiregrass regions struggled with low prices and limited markets. Tuskegee Institute graduates carried Carver's agricultural innovations into the countryside. In towns, Jim Crow signs defined every public space: separate water fountains, waiting rooms, schools, and cemeteries.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: 1901 Constitution disenfranchises virtually all Black voters and many poor whites
Convict leasing deaths continue — Banner Mine explosion kills dozens of leased convicts (1911, but conditions set in 1900s)
Boll weevil approaches Alabama, threatening the cotton economy
Lynching continues unabated — Alabama averages multiple racial killings per year
Industrial accidents in Birmingham mines and foundries
narrative_hooks: A Black farmer who voted in every election since Reconstruction walks to the registrar's office in 1902 and is turned away by a literacy test designed to be impossible to pass
A Black woman in Montgomery refuses to move to the back of a segregated streetcar in 1900 — decades before Rosa Parks, the struggle is already alive
A Birmingham coal miner descends into Pratt Mine at dawn, knowing that the convict laborers working beside him were leased by the state for the price of a mule
George Washington Carver teaches Black farmers at Tuskegee how to rotate crops and revive exhausted soil — science as resistance

key_events: Boll weevil devastates Alabama's cotton crop, beginning in the southern counties (1910s)
Great Migration begins in earnest — thousands of Black Alabamians head north for industrial jobs
World War I (1917–1918): Alabama sends approximately 75,000 soldiers; Camp Sheridan near Montgomery trains troops
Convict leasing finally abolished in Alabama (1928, but reform pressure builds in 1910s)
Alabama Dry law — prohibition enacted statewide (1915), ahead of national Prohibition
Flooding devastates Black Belt and river communities (1916)
1910s.economic_conditions: The boll weevil's arrival was an agricultural catastrophe. Cotton yields plummeted in affected counties, ruining sharecroppers and planters alike. Some farmers diversified into peanuts, pecans, and livestock — eventually, Dothan would erect a monument to the boll weevil for forcing diversification. World War I created industrial demand that pulled Black workers northward to better-paying factory jobs. Birmingham's steel industry benefited from wartime orders. But Alabama's fundamental economic structure — low wages, extractive industries, agricultural poverty — remained intact.
1910s.migration_patterns: The Great Migration transformed Alabama. Black families left the Black Belt, the wiregrass, and Birmingham by the thousands, heading for Chicago, Detroit, Cleveland, Pittsburgh, and other Northern industrial cities. The boll weevil and Jim Crow were twin push factors; Northern industrial wages were the pull. Within Alabama, rural-to-urban movement continued. World War I accelerated the migration — Northern factories desperate for labor actively recruited Black Southerners. White rural Alabamians also moved to mill towns and Birmingham.
1910s.social_context: The 1910s were marked by racial violence and the beginning of the Great Migration. Jim Crow was entrenched. Lynching continued. World War I created a paradox for Black Alabamians: fighting for democracy abroad while denied it at home. The war experience radicalized some Black veterans, planting seeds of future resistance. Alabama remained solidly Democratic and white supremacist. Labor organizing among Birmingham miners and steelworkers was brutally suppressed. Prohibition (1915) reflected the power of Protestant temperance movements.
1910s.daily_life_snapshot: For Black sharecroppers, the boll weevil was the last straw: after a ruined cotton crop, many packed what little they owned and bought train tickets north. Those who stayed faced the same grinding cycle of debt and labor. In Birmingham, steel and coal workers lived in company housing, shopped at company stores, and risked their lives in dangerous workplaces. World War I brought military camps and wartime activity. Women took on new roles as men went to war. In small towns, life revolved around church, courthouse, and cotton gin — now threatened by the weevil.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: Boll weevil devastation of cotton crops across Alabama (1910s onward)
Great Migration drains rural Black communities of their youngest and most ambitious
World War I casualties among Alabama servicemen
Severe flooding in the Black Belt and river valleys (1916)
Continued racial violence and lynching
narrative_hooks: A Black sharecropper looks at his weevil-eaten cotton and decides: Chicago cannot be worse than this
A Black soldier from Alabama trains at Camp Sheridan to fight in France — and wonders if the democracy he fights for will ever apply to him at home
A Black Belt planter watches the boll weevil destroy his crop and realizes that King Cotton's reign may be ending
A woman in Enterprise, Alabama picks up peanuts from the field where cotton used to grow, learning that survival means changing what you've always done

key_events: Great Migration continues — Alabama's Black population declines as thousands leave for Northern cities
