Ku Klux Klan resurges across Alabama, targeting not only Black citizens but also Catholics, Jews, and immigrants
Muscle Shoals controversy: debate over the Wilson Dam and nitrate plants built during WWI — Henry Ford bids to buy them (1920s)
Birmingham becomes the largest city in Alabama, driven by steel and coal
Dothan and the wiregrass region shift from cotton to peanuts and livestock
Hugo Black, future Supreme Court justice, joins the KKK to advance his political career in Alabama (1923)
1920s.economic_conditions: The 1920s were mixed for Alabama. Birmingham's industrial sector prospered, producing iron, steel, and coal for national markets. Agricultural Alabama struggled: the boll weevil continued destroying cotton, and low prices punished farmers who managed a crop. Diversification into peanuts, pecans, and livestock helped some counties. The state remained one of the poorest in the nation, with per capita income far below the national average. Sharecropping trapped rural families in poverty. The Muscle Shoals facility sat idle, awaiting resolution of its future.
dominant_industries: cotton (diminished by boll weevil and low prices)
1920s.migration_patterns: The Great Migration continued draining Alabama's Black rural communities. Entire families and sometimes whole communities relocated to Northern cities. Within Alabama, rural-to-Birmingham migration brought both Black and white workers to the industrial district. Some white hill country families moved to Huntsville, Gadsden, and textile mill towns. The Klan's resurgence made Alabama even more inhospitable to minorities. Italian, Greek, and Syrian communities in Birmingham maintained ethnic enclaves while assimilating into the broader white population.
1920s.social_context: The revived Ku Klux Klan was a powerful force in 1920s Alabama, claiming tens of thousands of members and influencing state politics. The Klan targeted not only Black citizens but also Catholics, Jews, immigrants, and anyone who challenged white Protestant dominance. Hugo Black's early KKK membership illustrated how mainstream the organization was. Jim Crow remained absolute — segregation governed every aspect of public life. Black Alabamians built parallel institutions: churches, schools, businesses, fraternal organizations. Birmingham's Black community developed a vibrant cultural life despite oppression.
1920s.daily_life_snapshot: In the 1920s, Alabama was two worlds. In Birmingham, the industrial city hummed with steel mills, coal mines, and railroads — workers lived in company towns, shopped at company stores, and breathed coal dust. In the countryside, sharecroppers still followed the cotton cycle, now complicated by the boll weevil. Electricity and automobiles reached some towns but bypassed most rural areas. Radio brought the outside world into Alabama homes for the first time. Jim Crow signs governed every public interaction between Black and white.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: KKK resurgence terrorizes minorities across Alabama (1920s)
Boll weevil continues devastating cotton crops
Rural poverty remains crushing — malnutrition, hookworm, pellagra are common
Industrial accidents in Birmingham mines and mills
Great Migration depletes Black communities of talent and labor
narrative_hooks: A Black family in the Black Belt sells their mule, packs a trunk, and boards a northbound train — leaving Alabama forever, they hope
A KKK rally in a Birmingham park draws thousands of white-robed men, and a Jewish shopkeeper watches from behind his curtain, wondering if this is still America
A Muscle Shoals farmer reads in the paper that Henry Ford wants to buy the Wilson Dam and imagines electricity and prosperity transforming his backwater county
A coal miner's wife in Walker County treats her husband's black lung cough with home remedies, knowing the company doctor will do nothing

key_events: Scottsboro Boys case (March 1931): nine Black teenagers falsely accused of rape; trials and appeals become an international cause celebre
Great Depression devastates Alabama — cotton prices fall to 5 cents a pound, mines close, unemployment soars
Tennessee Valley Authority (TVA) established (1933): Wilson Dam and new dams on the Tennessee River bring electricity and jobs to north Alabama
Civilian Conservation Corps (CCC) and Works Progress Administration (WPA) provide relief and employment
Share Croppers' Union organizes Black agricultural workers in Tallapoosa County; members shot and killed at Camp Hill (1931)
Birmingham steel industry contracts severely — unemployment exceeds 25%
1930s.economic_conditions: The Great Depression hit Alabama harder than most states because Alabama was already poor. Cotton prices collapsed to levels that could not sustain sharecropping families. Birmingham's steel industry contracted sharply, throwing thousands out of work. Coal mines closed. The New Deal provided critical relief: TVA brought electricity, flood control, and jobs to the Tennessee Valley; CCC and WPA employed thousands building roads, schools, and public buildings. But structural poverty persisted — Alabama remained one of the poorest states in the nation.
1930s.migration_patterns: The Great Migration slowed during the Depression — Northern factory jobs disappeared. Some Black Alabamians who had migrated north returned south when they could not find work. Within Alabama, desperate rural families moved to towns seeking any work. TVA construction drew workers to north Alabama. CCC camps employed young men across the state. Despite the Depression, the fundamental push factors — Jim Crow, poverty, violence — remained, and migration would resume with the next economic upturn.
1930s.social_context: The Scottsboro Boys case exposed Alabama's racial injustice to the world. Nine Black teenagers, falsely accused and convicted by all-white juries, became an international symbol of Jim Crow's lethal cruelty. The Communist Party and NAACP competed to represent them. The Share Croppers' Union's suppression in Tallapoosa County showed the violence that met any challenge to the racial and economic order. The New Deal brought federal power into Alabama in unprecedented ways — TVA, WPA, CCC — but left the Jim Crow system intact. Racial segregation continued in all New Deal programs.
1930s.daily_life_snapshot: The 1930s were desperate years. Black Belt sharecroppers ate corn, collard greens, fatback, and whatever they could grow or forage. Pellagra, hookworm, and malaria were epidemic among the rural poor. In Birmingham, unemployed steel workers and miners lined up at soup kitchens. CCC boys planted trees, built trails, and earned $30 a month — $25 of which was sent home to families. TVA brought electric light to Tennessee Valley homes for the first time. Radio was the universal entertainment — families gathered to listen to the Grand Ole Opry, FDR's Fireside Chats, and the Scottsboro trial coverage.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: Scottsboro Boys falsely convicted — a stain on Alabama justice (1931 onward)
Great Depression causes mass unemployment, hunger, and poverty
Share Croppers' Union members killed by vigilantes at Camp Hill (1931)
Pellagra, hookworm, and malnutrition epidemic among rural poor
Birmingham unemployment exceeds 25%; steel mills operate far below capacity
narrative_hooks: One of the Scottsboro Boys, a teenager riding a freight train looking for work, is pulled off by a mob and charged with a crime he did not commit — his life will never be the same
A Black Belt sharecropper's family eats their last cornmeal and wonders where tomorrow's food will come from
A TVA worker helps pour concrete for a Tennessee River dam, knowing that the electricity it generates will change north Alabama forever
A Share Croppers' Union organizer meets in secret in a Black church in Tallapoosa County, knowing that the men with guns are never far away

key_events: World War II transforms Alabama's economy — military bases and defense plants proliferate
Redstone Arsenal established near Huntsville for ordnance research (1941)
Brookley Air Force Base in Mobile and Craig Air Force Base in Selma expand military presence
Alabama Dry Dock and Shipbuilding Company in Mobile employs thousands, including Black workers — racial violence erupts (May 1943)
Black veterans return from war demanding equal rights; voter registration efforts begin
Tuskegee Airmen — the 332nd Fighter Group — train at Tuskegee Army Air Field, becoming the most decorated unit in the Army Air Corps
1940s.economic_conditions: World War II ended the Great Depression in Alabama. Military bases — Redstone Arsenal, Brookley Field, Maxwell Field, Fort McClellan, Fort Rucker — brought federal spending and jobs. Defense plants hired workers of both races, though Black workers were confined to the worst jobs and paid less. Shipbuilding in Mobile boomed. Agriculture mechanized gradually, reducing the need for sharecropping labor and accelerating rural depopulation. Alabama's economy began transitioning from agriculture to military-industrial dependence.
1940s.migration_patterns: World War II accelerated the Great Migration. Black Alabamians moved north in unprecedented numbers, drawn by defense industry jobs in Detroit, Chicago, and other cities. Within Alabama, rural workers — Black and white — moved to military bases and defense plants. Mobile's population surged with shipyard workers. The Tuskegee Airmen's presence brought national attention to the small town. After the war, Black veterans returned with expectations of equality that Alabama's Jim Crow system could not fulfill — setting the stage for the civil rights movement.
1940s.social_context: The war exposed Alabama's racial contradictions more sharply than ever. Black soldiers fought fascism abroad while enduring Jim Crow at home. The Tuskegee Airmen proved that Black pilots could fly and fight as well as anyone — undermining the rationale for segregation. The 1943 Mobile shipyard riot, when white workers attacked Black colleagues who had been promoted, showed the volatility of racial change in the workplace. Black veterans returned home radicalized, determined to claim the rights they had fought for. The NAACP grew in Alabama. The postwar period was a pressure cooker.
1940s.daily_life_snapshot: Wartime Alabama was energized and anxious. Workers poured into Mobile, Huntsville, and other military towns, living in hastily built housing. Women entered the workforce in new numbers — 'Rosie the Riveter' had an Alabama accent too. Rationing limited food, fuel, and consumer goods. Black soldiers at Tuskegee Army Air Field trained under the double burden of military discipline and Jim Crow. In the countryside, older farmers and women kept cotton and food crops going while young men fought overseas. Gold star banners hung in windows across the state.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: Mobile shipyard race riot (May 1943) — white workers attack Black colleagues
World War II casualties among Alabama servicemen
Continued Jim Crow segregation in military bases and defense plants
Housing shortages in military boom towns
Rural depopulation as workers leave farms for military-industrial jobs
narrative_hooks: A Tuskegee Airman straps into his P-51 Mustang, knowing his skill in the cockpit challenges everything white Alabama believes about his race
A Black shipyard worker in Mobile is promoted — and white coworkers attack him with pipes and wrenches in the 1943 riot
A Black veteran steps off the train in Montgomery after V-J Day, wearing his uniform and medals, and is told to use the colored entrance
A farm wife in the Black Belt reads the telegram from the War Department and knows, before opening it, what it says about her son

key_events: Brown v. Board of Education (1954): Supreme Court rules school segregation unconstitutional — Alabama resists
Rosa Parks refuses to give up her bus seat in Montgomery (December 1, 1955)
Montgomery Bus Boycott (December 1955 – December 1956): 381-day boycott led by Martin Luther King Jr. and the Montgomery Improvement Association desegregates city buses
Autherine Lucy admitted to the University of Alabama; mob violence forces her suspension (February 1956)
Alabama legislature passes Massive Resistance laws to prevent school desegregation
Wernher von Braun and German rocket scientists at Redstone Arsenal develop missiles; Huntsville becomes 'Rocket City'
NAACP banned in Alabama by court order (1956); the organization fights for years to regain legal status
1950s.economic_conditions: The 1950s brought economic modernization alongside racial conflict. Huntsville's transformation into a space and missile center — driven by Wernher von Braun and the Army Ballistic Missile Agency — created a high-tech economy in north Alabama. Birmingham's steel industry remained important but faced increasing competition. Military bases continued providing economic stability. Cotton's decline accelerated as mechanization eliminated sharecropping jobs. The state's political leaders invested more energy in fighting desegregation than in economic development.
1950s.migration_patterns: The Great Migration continued. Black Alabamians left for Northern and Western cities in large numbers, pushed by Jim Crow and pulled by economic opportunity. Within Alabama, rural-to-urban migration accelerated as cotton mechanization eliminated sharecropping jobs. Huntsville attracted scientists, engineers, and technical workers — many from outside the South — creating an unusually cosmopolitan enclave. Birmingham's growth slowed as steel faced national competition. Mobile's military economy stabilized.
1950s.social_context: The Montgomery Bus Boycott was the opening salvo of the modern civil rights movement. Rosa Parks' arrest, the emergence of Martin Luther King Jr. as a national leader, and the 381-day boycott demonstrated that Black Southerners could organize, sustain protest, and win. Alabama's white political establishment responded with Massive Resistance — banning the NAACP, passing segregation laws, and using violence and intimidation. Autherine Lucy's experience at the University of Alabama showed the depth of white resistance. The decade was a gathering storm.
1950s.daily_life_snapshot: Alabama in the 1950s was a state in tension. In Montgomery, Black citizens walked miles to work during the bus boycott rather than ride segregated buses — an act of collective discipline that amazed the nation. In Birmingham, steel workers and their families lived in segregated neighborhoods defined by race and class. In Huntsville, rocket scientists and engineers created a futuristic world amid old Southern culture. In the Black Belt, mechanization was emptying the countryside — tractors replaced mules and sharecroppers. Television brought images of the boycott and the wider world into Alabama homes.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: Massive Resistance to desegregation — NAACP banned, schools defiant
Violence against civil rights activists — bombings, beatings, intimidation
Autherine Lucy expelled from the University of Alabama under mob pressure (1956)
Continued economic decline in rural Black Belt and wiregrass counties
Cotton mechanization displaces thousands of rural workers
narrative_hooks: Rosa Parks sits down on a Montgomery bus on December 1, 1955, and refuses to get up — and an entire movement rises with her
A Black domestic worker in Montgomery walks five miles to work during the bus boycott, her feet aching but her dignity intact
Autherine Lucy walks through a howling white mob to attend class at the University of Alabama — and the university suspends her for her own 'safety'
A German rocket scientist at Redstone Arsenal works on missiles that may reach the moon, while just outside the gate, Jim Crow rules apply
A sharecropper family loads their truck and leaves the Black Belt for Detroit, watching Alabama disappear in the rearview mirror

key_events: Freedom Riders attacked by mobs in Anniston and Birmingham (May 1961) — bus firebombed in Anniston
Birmingham Campaign (April–May 1963): Martin Luther King Jr. leads demonstrations; Commissioner Bull Connor orders fire hoses and police dogs turned on peaceful protesters, including children
16th Street Baptist Church bombing in Birmingham kills four girls — Addie Mae Collins, Cynthia Wesley, Carole Robertson, and Carol Denise McNair (September 15, 1963)
Governor George Wallace 'stands in the schoolhouse door' at the University of Alabama to block desegregation (June 11, 1963)
Selma to Montgomery marches: 'Bloody Sunday' on the Edmund Pettus Bridge (March 7, 1965) — state troopers attack peaceful marchers
Voting Rights Act of 1965 signed into law, directly responding to Selma — transforms Black political power in Alabama
Vivian Malone and James Hood desegregate the University of Alabama (June 1963)
George Wallace runs for president on a segregationist platform (1964, 1968)
1960s.economic_conditions: The 1960s brought economic change alongside social revolution. Huntsville's space program boomed — the Saturn V rocket that would take astronauts to the moon was developed at Marshall Space Flight Center. Birmingham's steel industry continued its long decline, but the city diversified into banking, medicine, and education. The Voting Rights Act began opening economic as well as political doors for Black Alabamians. Rural Alabama continued losing population. Military bases remained economic anchors. Alabama's reputation for racial violence deterred some investment.
dominant_industries: aerospace and space program (Huntsville — Saturn V, Marshall Space Flight Center)
1960s.migration_patterns: The Great Migration continued, though at a slower pace as civil rights gains made the South marginally more livable. Birmingham lost Black residents to Northern cities. Huntsville attracted technical workers from across the nation. Rural depopulation continued across the Black Belt and hill country. Some white Alabamians migrated to suburbs and exurbs to avoid desegregation. The Voting Rights Act began changing the calculus — for the first time since Reconstruction, Black Alabamians had a political reason to stay.
1960s.social_context: The 1960s were Alabama's most consequential decade since the Civil War. The civil rights movement's most iconic moments occurred in Alabama: the Freedom Rides, the Birmingham Campaign, the 16th Street Baptist Church bombing, the Selma marches. Bull Connor's fire hoses and George Wallace's defiance became symbols of white supremacy's last stand. But the courage of ordinary Black Alabamians — children marching in Birmingham, John Lewis on the Edmund Pettus Bridge, thousands registering to vote — changed America. The Voting Rights Act of 1965 was the direct result of Alabama's struggle.
1960s.daily_life_snapshot: The 1960s in Alabama were a time of confrontation and transformation. In Birmingham, Black children marched out of 16th Street Baptist Church to face fire hoses and police dogs — and their courage was broadcast on national television. In Selma, marchers crossed the Edmund Pettus Bridge into a wall of state trooper violence. In Huntsville, engineers built rockets for the moon while the state convulsed with racial conflict. In small towns, Black citizens quietly registered to vote for the first time in their lives. Jim Crow signs came down — slowly, grudgingly, but they came down.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: 16th Street Baptist Church bombing kills four girls (September 15, 1963)
Freedom Rider bus firebombed in Anniston (May 14, 1961)
Bloody Sunday: state troopers attack Selma marchers on Edmund Pettus Bridge (March 7, 1965)
Murder of civil rights workers — Jimmie Lee Jackson (February 1965), Viola Liuzzo (March 1965), Jonathan Daniels (August 1965)
George Wallace's inflammatory rhetoric fuels national division
Continued economic decline in rural areas
narrative_hooks: A fourteen-year-old girl marches out of 16th Street Baptist Church in Birmingham, facing fire hoses and police dogs, because she believes that children can change the world
Four girls are killed by a bomb at 16th Street Baptist Church on a Sunday morning — and a nation that had looked away from Alabama can look away no longer
John Lewis, twenty-five years old, walks onto the Edmund Pettus Bridge in Selma knowing that the troopers ahead intend to hurt him — and walks forward anyway
A Black grandmother in Dallas County, Alabama, registers to vote in 1965 — the first time in her life anyone has allowed her to exercise that right
A Freedom Rider watches his bus burn in Anniston and realizes that the cost of integration may be higher than anyone imagined
An engineer at Marshall Space Flight Center in Huntsville watches the Saturn V rise on a pillar of flame, while fifty miles south, churches burn for a different reason
